prompts.name: Skill Factoring and Boundary Refactorer
prompts.sprintRole: Factor oversized, overlapping, or prompt-only skills into deterministic reusable skill boundaries
prompts.useWhen: A repository has one or more skills that are too broad, duplicate other skills, hide application behavior in prose, lack deterministic triggers, or cannot be validated independently.
prompts.inspectFirst: AGENTS.md, HARNESS.md, SKILLS.md, CAPABILITIES.md, TRIGGERS.md, .ai/skills, skill manifests, routing tables, application code, validators, fixtures, open PRs, and recent commits.
prompts.expectedOutput: A committed skill-factoring change that classifies each inspected skill as keep, split, merge, retire, or rewire; preserves reusable guidance; moves deterministic behavior into code/contracts; updates triggers and manifests; and adds focused validation.
prompts.nextStep: Run P62 when the factored skill boundaries need executable quality, routing, correctness, performance, and token-efficiency evals.
prompts.proofGate: Every changed skill has a deterministic activation condition, explicit inputs and outputs, forbidden conditions, owning files, tests or fixtures, and a commit ahead of the recorded floor.
prompts.copyContent:
FACTOR THE REPOSITORY SKILLS NOW. DO NOT STOP AT A TAXONOMY, INVENTORY, OR PLAN.

Repo: resolve the current repository from the environment.
Branch: preserve existing work and use an isolated branch or worktree.
Sprint: Skill Factoring and Boundary Refactor.
Owned scope: skill files, skill manifests, deterministic triggers, capability references, routing contracts, focused validators, fixtures, and directly related docs.
Forbidden scope: unrelated product behavior, secret handling, provider authentication, destructive cleanup, force-push, and moving application logic into prompts.

1. Inspect repository law, skill files, capabilities, triggers, routing, application code, validators, fixtures, open PRs, and recent history.
2. Build a disposition table for every in-scope skill: KEEP, SPLIT, MERGE, RETIRE, or REWIRE.
3. For every retained or created skill, define one deterministic trigger, required inputs, produced outputs, preconditions, forbidden conditions, guardrails, owning files, and proof ceiling.
4. Split skills when one file mixes unrelated domains, multiple activation conditions, or application behavior with procedural guidance.
5. Merge skills only when their triggers, inputs, outputs, and validation are materially identical.
6. Move deterministic behavior into scripts, modules, schemas, registries, or workflows. Skills may explain judgment and procedure but must not be the only implementation of product behavior.
7. Update skill manifests, trigger routing, capability references, and operator documentation so a fresh agent can select the correct skill without guessing.
8. Add positive trigger fixtures, negative trigger fixtures, boundary tests, and a validator that rejects duplicate ownership or ambiguous activation.
9. Run focused skill and routing checks, existing repository validators, build/static checks, secret and generated-output hygiene, and git diff --check.
10. Commit the coherent factoring change and push or open/update a PR when allowed.

FINAL REPORT
Repo, branch, skill disposition table, files, trigger and capability changes, validators, fixtures, commit SHA, push or PR state, proof achieved, proof ceiling, final Git status, and one exact next command.

prompts.name: Skill Correctness and Efficiency Eval Implementer
prompts.sprintRole: Install executable evals that expose skill weaknesses, guide repairs, prove correctness, and measure performance and token efficiency
prompts.useWhen: A repository skill exists or has been factored, but bugs, missing functionality, routing quality, integration behavior, regression safety, latency, cost, tool use, or AI token efficiency are not proven by executable evals.
prompts.inspectFirst: AGENTS.md; the target skill; skill, trigger, capability, routing, workflow, and artifact contracts; existing unit, integration, regression, performance, and eval runners; representative tasks; historical defects; output schemas; token/cost telemetry; proof vocabulary; CI conventions; open PRs; and recent commits.
prompts.expectedOutput: A committed skill-eval system with a versioned case matrix, deterministic unit and integration assertions, positive/negative/boundary/regression fixtures, weakness and repair ledger, reproducible baseline, performance and token instrumentation, machine-readable before/after results, focused CI, and any directly justified skill or factoring repairs.
prompts.nextStep: Run the installed eval runner against the exact target skill commit, repair every valid failing case without weakening the contract, compare correctness and efficiency to the recorded baseline, and open or print the canonical machine-readable report.
prompts.proofGate: Every required case runs reproducibly; deterministic correctness and safety failures block CI; unit, integration, boundary, malformed-input, and regression coverage exists; measured token/latency/cost improvements preserve quality; findings map to repairs or explicit blockers; results are machine-readable; and no static or synthetic check is mislabeled as provider, runtime, or production proof.
